title: Japan Prepares 10-Player Backup List for World Cup
original_text:
[Sports Chosun Reporter Park Sang-kyung] Japan is sharpening its blade.
Sports Nippon reported on the 19th that Japan, which announced its final roster for the 2026 North America World Cup, is preparing a 10-member backup list. Citing multiple sources, the newspaper stated, "Japan has secured a backup list to replace injured players such as Soma Yuki, Matsuda Zebra, Ando Tomoya, St. Pauli, and Sano Kodai, NEC Nijmegen, preparing for variables."
The International Football Association (FIFA) allows substitutions for players in the 26-member final roster who cannot participate due to illness or injury up to 24 hours before the first match. Sports Nippon revealed, "The Japan Football Association (JFA) has been planning a backup list of around 10 players, primarily from the squad that traveled to England in March."
Head coach Hajime Moriyasu, during the final roster announcement press conference in Tokyo on the 15th, mentioned the backup members, saying, "Although not officially announced, the selected players have been informed and are preparing." Sports Nippon added, "Coach Moriyasu requested the players to maintain their condition."
Most World Cup participants prepare backup lists to address potential variables in their final rosters. Hong Myung-bo’s team, heading to North America, is no exception.
Despite this, Japan’s early and extensive preparation for a backup list stems from past injury traumas.
Significant players in the final roster, including Endo Wataru (Liverpool), Tomiyasu Takehiro (Ajax), and Suzuki Yuito (Freiburg), either have injury histories or are returning from recovery. While their current condition is deemed suitable for the tournament, the risk of re-injury remains. During the 2022 Qatar tournament, Japan’s defender Nakayama Yuya withdrew due to an Achilles tendon injury, and Machino Shuto was called up as a replacement.
Meanwhile, Takumi Minamino (AS Monaco), excluded from the final roster due to injury, will join the team as a helper. Sports Nippon reported, "The JFA received permission from Monaco for Minamino to participate as a training backup player and plans to assign a dedicated medical staff to assist his rehabilitation."
Reporter Park Sang-kyung ppark@sportschosun.com
· This article has been translated by Upstage Solar AI.
